definition: The Kalecki-Levy profit equation shows fiscal deficits mechanically support corporate earnings. The US 6-7% deficit contributes an estimated $20-40/share of S&P 500 EPS (7-15%). Any fiscal consolidation sufficient to stabilize debt/GDP would directly reduce the earnings base, creating a consolidation paradox where fiscal improvement triggers earnings recession.
evidence: Reverse scenario: deficit narrowing from 6.5% to 3.5% GDP mechanically reduces EPS ~6-10% but enables multiple expansion
Accounting identity: fiscal deficit flows into corporate profits after savings offset and net export leakage
~25-35% of S&P 500 net income attributable to deficit channel
Reverse scenario analysis: deficit narrowing trades ~6-10% EPS decline for 2-4x multiple expansion
Equity analysts seeing resilient earnings as bullish ignore that the earnings source compresses the multiple
Kalecki-Levy profit equation is an accounting identity (direction certain)
1993-2000 counter-case: deficit reduction coexisted with earnings growth during productivity boom
Compounding four +/-30% uncertainties produces essentially directional estimate
open_questions: Would multiple expansion from negative correlation reassertion fully offset EPS decline?
How quickly would the earnings channel respond to fiscal consolidation?
What is the precise savings rate offset and corporate profit share (±30% uncertainty)?
How quickly would multiple expansion offset EPS decline under deficit narrowing?
Does the Kalecki-Levy identity hold at this scale in the current financial system?
Is the fiscal earnings support temporary or structural?
Does the 1993-2000 productivity boom experience generalize to current conditions?
Can the Kalecki-Levy magnitude be narrowed with better sectoral data?
